channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: U.S.P.S. Postmaster Says Service Will Be ‘Out of Cash’ in Under a Year
item.description: The U.S.P.S. leader said at a hearing that the service could run out of cash within a year and asked lawmakers to increase its borrowing limits.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Cliff Owen/Associated Press
item.media:description: Postmaster General David Steiner

item.title: U.S. Says Anthropic Is an ‘Unacceptable’ National Security Risk
item.description: In a legal filing, the government said it questioned whether the A.I. start-up could be a “trusted partner” in wartime, which led it to label the company a supply chain risk.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic has accused the Pentagon of labeling it a “supply chain risk” to punish it on ideological grounds.

item.title: Judge Orders Voice of America to Restart All News Operations
item.description: More than 1,000 full-time journalists and support staff at the news group were ordered to return to work by March 23 and to resume broadcasting.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Agency for Global Media
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: A federal judge’s ruling orders more than 1,000 full-time workers and support staff to return to work at Voice of America by March 23.

item.title: Year After Trump Takeover, Institute of Peace Is Little More Than Scenery
item.description: As a lawsuit by the former staff drags on, the fight highlights President Trump’s upending of traditional peace-building and the lasting effects of his administration’s cost-cutting blitz.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Institute of Peace was recently renamed the Donald J. Trump United States Institute of Peace.

item.title: In His Resignation Letter, Joe Kent Spoke About the Death of His Wife
item.description: Chief Petty Officer Shannon Kent was killed in action during a special operations mission in Syria in 2019. Mr. Kent said he could not support “sending the next generation off to fight and die” in Iran.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jenny Kane/Associated Press
item.media:description: Joe Kent, the director of the National Counterterrorism Center, resigned on Tuesday.

item.title: No Trump Endorsement for Cornyn or Paxton in Texas Senate Race as Deadline Passes
item.description: President Trump’s decision not to weigh in before the deadline means both John Cornyn and Ken Paxton remain on the ballot, extending their costly and increasingly personal battle into a May runoff.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Al Drago, Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times

item.title: Trump’s Next Decision in War: Whether to Retrieve Iran’s Nuclear Fuel
item.description: A mission to seize or destroy Iran’s nuclear material would be one of the riskiest military operations in modern American history.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Vantor, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A satellite image of the Pickaxe Mountain tunnel complex near the Natanz nuclear facility in central Iran last week. It is not certain where all of Iran’s nuclear material resides.

item.title: Kharg Island Is an Appealing Target for Trump, With High Risks
item.description: A U.S. attack or a move to seize control of Iran’s main oil export hub could cripple the country’s ability to profit from its natural resources. But it would also risk sending energy prices even higher.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski, Rebecca F. Elliott and Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Atta Kenare/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Kharg Island oil facility off the coast of Iran in 2017.

item.title: Trump Administration Offers Narrow Immigration Changes to End D.H.S. Shutdown
item.description: Administration officials have put forth several proposals to answer Democratic demands for major changes in President Trump’s crackdown in exchange for reopening the Department of Homeland Security.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Border Patrol agents in Minneapolis earlier this year. The White House’s letter did not address barring federal immigration officers from wearing masks, something that Democrats have demanded.

item.title: Miami Cubans Fear Trump Won’t Go Far Enough: ‘The Castros Have to Go’
item.description: Many Cubans in Florida have sought regime change for decades. They fear that President Trump’s talks with Cuba will not lead to wholesale political transformation.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Marco Bello/Reuters
item.media:description: Supporters of President Trump protesting Cuba’s government in Miami in February.

item.title: Bipartisan Bill Would Waive $100,000 H-1B Visa Fees for Doctors and Nurses
item.description: The bill, sponsored by two Republicans and two Democrats in the House, seeks to alleviate shortages of doctors and nurses in the U.S.
item.dc:creator: Roni Caryn Rabin
item.media:credit: Morgan Lieberman for The New York Times
item.media:description: U.S. teaching hospitals rely heavily on newly minted foreign medical school graduates.

item.title: Chief Justice Says Personal Attacks on Judges Are ‘Dangerous’ and Must Stop
item.description: The public remarks from Chief Justice John Roberts were his first since President Trump excoriated the justices who ruled against his tariffs in harsh and personal terms.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Chief Justice John G. Roberts Jr. during the State of the Union address last month. He has previously objected to attacks on judges, but primarily in writing.

item.title: Trump Says U.S. Doesn’t Need Allies to Reopen Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump has faced mounting criticism over the U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran from allies and even members of his own administration.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump spoke to reporters on Tuesday while meeting with Micheál Martin, the prime minister of Ireland, in the Oval Office.

item.title: Flight Prices Are Up Amid Soaring Fuel Costs, but Travel Demand Remains High
item.description: Airline executives said the war in Iran had raised fuel costs and, as a result, fares, but that had not yet hurt demand for tickets from individuals and businesses.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Aaron Schwartz/Reuters
item.media:description: For airlines, geopolitical shocks like the one caused by the Iran war can send operating costs soaring.

item.title: Trump’s Gutting of Election Security Fuels Worries for Midterms
item.description: Officials say the crippling of the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, which the president himself created, could open elections to cyberattacks and foreign influence.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Zaydee Sanchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency was created to protect critical infrastructure, including elections, from cyberthreats.

item.title: What’s in Trump’s SAVE Act, the Voter ID Bill Republicans Are Pushing?
item.description: The legislation would require voters to prove their citizenship in person upon registration, ban IDs without a photo at polling places and criminalize failures to enforce such requirements.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: A polling station in New York City during a mayoral election last year.

item.title: Joe Kent, a Top Counterterrorism Official for the Trump Administration, Resigns, Citing Iran War
item.description: Mr. Kent is the highest-ranking Trump administration official to quit in opposition to the conflict. He is also known for his support of conspiracy theories.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Robert Draper and Luke Broadwater
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jenny Kane/Associated Press
item.media:description: “I cannot in good conscience support the ongoing war in Iran,” Joe Kent, the director of the National Counterterrorism Center, wrote in a letter to President Trump that he posted Tuesday.

item.title: In Maine, Janet Mills Slams Graham Platner in First Negative Ad
item.description: Facing a tough nomination fight in a major Senate race, Ms. Mills, the state’s governor, is attacking her Democratic rival over his old Reddit posts and a tattoo that resembled Nazi imagery.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Janet Mills of Maine is backed by Senator Chuck Schumer, the minority leader, but polls suggest that her Democratic rival, Graham Platner, began this year with an advantage.

item.title: Admiral’s Comments Undercut Pentagon’s Cluster Munition Policy
item.description: The first Trump administration defended cluster munitions as “legitimate,” but on Monday, Adm. Brad Cooper condemned them as “inherently indiscriminate.”
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times
item.media:description: Adm. Brad Cooper, the leader of U.S. Central Command, in November.

item.title: Senate Takes Up Voter Bill Sought by Trump but Opposed by Democrats
item.description: The Republican leader said he couldn’t overcome a filibuster but, under relentless pressure from the president and the far right, would put Democrats on the record against the restrictive voter I.D. bill.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Thune, the majority leader, has indicated that he will allow the voter measure to be debated for days, though he suggested that it would still ultimately be subject to the 60-vote threshold to cut off debate.

item.title: Trump Officials Weigh New Plan to Stop Offshore Wind Farms
item.description: Proposed settlements would block wind farms off New York State and North Carolina, according to documents reviewed by The New York Times.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.category: Bureau of Ocean Energy Management, Regulation and Enforcement
item.media:credit: Stephane Mahe/Reuters
item.media:description: Under proposed settlements, the Interior Department would cancel the leases for two offshore wind projects, while the Justice Department would pay more than $928 million to the developers to reimburse them for their winning bids in lease sales.

item.title: War in Iran Shows Growing Conservative Divide Over Israel
item.description: As the U.S.-Israel-Iran war continues, conservatism’s most famous figures are in a rhetorical brawl over America’s role.
item.dc:creator: Richard Fausset and Ken Bensinger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times; Adriana Zehbrauskas for The New York Times
